When making its way among the branches, it occasionally inclines the body to either side, so as alternately to shew its whole upper or under parts. "I could not think of any better mode of representing these birds than that which I have adopted, as it exhibits them in the exercise of their nefarious propensities. Look at them: The male, as if full of delight at the sight of the havoc which he has already committed on the tender, juicy, unripe corn on which he stands, has swelled his throat, and is calling in exultation to his companions to come and assist him in demolishing it. The female has fed herself, and is about to fly off with a well-loaded bill to her hungry and expectant brood, that, from the nest, look on their plundering parents, joyously anticipating the pleasures of which they shall ere long be allowed to participate. See how torn the husk is from the ear, and how nearly devoured the grains of corn already are! This is the tithe our Blackbirds take from our planters and farmers; but it was so appointed, and such is the will of the beneficent Creator." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"The common name given to this bird in the Eastern and Middle Districts of our Union is that of Quail, but in the Western and Southern States, the more appropriate appellation of Partridge is bestowed upon it. It is abundantly met with in all parts of the United States, but more especially towards the interior... The flight of these birds is generally performed at a short distance from the ground. It is rapid, and is continued by numerous quick flaps of the wings for a certain distance, after which the bird sails until about to alight, when again it flaps its wings to break its descent. When chased by dogs, or started by any other enemy, they fly to the middle branches of trees of ordinary size, where they remain until danger is over. They walk with ease on the branches. If they perceive that they are observed, they raise the feathers of their head, emit a low note, and fly off either to some higher branch of the same tree, or to another tree at a distance. When these birds rise on wing of their own accord, the whole flock takes the same course; but when put up (in the sportsman's phrase), they disperse, after alighting call to each other, and soon after unite, each running or flying towards the well-known cry of the patriarch of the covey. " - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"The Goshawk is of rare occurrence in most parts of the United States, and the districts of North America to which it usually retires to breed are as yet unknown. Some individuals nestle within the Union, others in the British provinces of New Brunswick and Nova Scotia, but the greater part seem to proceed farther north. I saw none, however, in Labrador, but was informed that they are plentiful in the wooded parts of Newfoundland. On returning from the north, they make their appearance in the Middle States about the beginning of September, and after that season range to very great distances. I have found them rather abundant in the lower parts of Kentucky and Indiana, and in severe winters I have seen a few even in Louisiana. In the Great Pine Forest of Pennsylvania, and at the Falls of Niagara, I have observed them breeding. During autumn and winter, they are common in Maine, as well as in Nova Scotia, where I have seen six or seven specimens that were procured by a single person in the course of a season." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"On the 24th November of that year, in the course of an excursion near the town of St. Augustine, I observed a bird flying at a great elevation, and almost over my head. Convinced that it was unknown to me, and bent on obtaining it, I followed it nearly a mile, when I saw it sail towards the earth, making for a place where a group of Vultures were engaged in devouring a dead horse. Walking up to the horse, I observed the new bird alighted on it, and helping itself freely to the savoury meat beneath its feet; but it evinced a decree of shyness far greater than that of its associates, the Turkey-Buzzards and Carrion Crows." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"As the Marion was nearing the curious islets of the Tortugas, one of the birds that more particularly attracted my notice was of this species. The nearer we approached the land, the more numerous did they become, and I felt delighted with the hope that ere many days should elapse, I should have an opportunity of studying their habits. As night drew her sombre curtain over the face of nature, some of these birds alighted on the top-yard of our bark, and I observed ever afterwards that they manifested a propensity to roost at as great a height as possible above the surrounding objects, making choice of the tops of bushes, or even upright poles, and disputing with each other the privilege" (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"This species is more generally known than any of our Herons, it being very extensively dispersed in spring, summer, and early autumn. It ranges along our many rivers to great distances from the sea, being common on the Missouri and its branches, from which it spreads to all such localities as are favourable to its habits. To the north of the United States, however, it is very seldom seen, it being of rare occurrence even in Nova Scotia. At the approach of winter it retires to the Floridas and Lower Louisiana, where individuals, however, reside all the year, and many remove southward beyond the limits of our country." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

The species may well have been a natural vagrant to the Gulf Coast in the 19th century or earlier - in The Birds of America, John James Audubon made brief remarks regarding three rubra specimens he encountered in Louisiana, but rarely found today. "It was supposed by WILSON, and since his time by others, that this brilliantly coloured Ibis is not uncommon in the southern parts of the United States. This opinion, however, is quite erroneous, and I have found the Scarlet Ibis less numerous than even the Glossy Ibis; indeed I have not met with more than three individuals in a state of liberty, in the whole range of the United States. These birds occurred at Bayou Sara, in Louisiana, on the 3d of July, 1821. They were travelling in a line, in the manner of the White Ibis, above the tops of the trees. Although I had only a glimpse of them, I saw them sufficiently well to be assured of their belonging to the present species, and therefore I have thought it proper to introduce it into our Fauna. WILSON'S figure, I believe, was taken from a living specimen, not, however, procured within the limits of the United States, and which was kept for some time in Peale's Museum in Philadelphia. My drawing of the adult male, and that of the immature bird, were made from specimens also procured beyond our limits. It is said that the habits of this bird are very similar to those of the White Ibis, of which I give you a long account; but, as I have not had opportunities of observing them, I judge it better to abstain from offering any remarks on this subject." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

"The flight of this species is undulating, firm, tolerably swift, and capable of being protracted over a large space. While travelling they pass in the air in straggling flocks, and keep up a constant noise, each individual now and then emitting a clear note or call. They move with ease on the ground, alight sidewise on the walls of houses and on trees, on the twigs of which they climb with the aid of their bill. When caged they soon become tame, and are fed without any difficulty." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831). ----------- Specially Colored for William IV King of England 1830-1837. The set of Audubon's The Birds of America belonging to the Ducal House of Sachsen-Meiningen is closely tied to the history of Europe of the past two centuries. The story begins with its subscription by Queen Adelaide of England, nei Adelheid Princess of Sachsen-Meiningen, and ends with its sale at auction on June 25 in New York. In between lie eras of change with ups and downs, which, especially in the 20th century, have had a lasting effect on the living members of the family. The House of Sachsen-Meiningen ruled from the 17th Century until 1918 and among others shaped the political, economic and cultural development of Thuringia. During the reign of the broad-minded Duke Georg II (1826-1914), the so-called "Theater Duke" ("Theater Herzog"), the ducal capital of Meiningen became a center of European theater and music in the 19th century. Of equal importance was the preservation and expansion of the art collection and library, which was opened to the public at an early stage. Georg's extraordinary artistic talent was passed on through the family, continued most notably by the line of the Barons von Saalfeld which was established by his son Ernst. Members of the Ducal Family were seriously affected by the political upheaval in the Communist controlled part of Germany after 1945 and lost their ancestral home and their possessions. After the reunification of Germany in 1990, the family was faced with a completely altered political situation. In the ancestral tradition of support and patronage of the arts, the family waived its rights to large portions of the art collection for the benefit of the Cultural Foundation of Meiningen (Kulturstiftung Meiningen) and will participate in the future in the cultural development of Meiningen by holding a permanent seat on the Board of Trustees of the Foundation. As compensation for waiving its claim to the ducal property, the magnificent Audubon plates, among other works, were returned to the family.

Audubon Aquatint - California Vulture, Plate 426 AUDUBON, John James (1785 - 1851). California Vulture, Plate 426. Aquatint engraving with original hand color. London: Robert Havell, 1827-1838. 38 1/4" x 25 1/2" sheet. Comparables: Arader Galleries, 2024 - $43,200 (Sachsen-Meiningen); Arader Galleries, 2021 - $18,000. The California Condor is one of the greatest conservation stories in American history. After recovering from near extinction, the birds are protected under the Endangered Species Act and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act. From just 27 birds in 1987, the species was brought back from the brink through an extraordinary rescue effort involving captive breeding, protection of fragile DDT-weakened eggs, and careful reintroduction to the wild. Today, more than 500 condors soar once again over the western skies. Audubon's majestic portrait, here titled California Vulture, captures the immense presence and commanding form of North America's largest flying bird. "Of the three species of Vulture which inhabit the southern parts of North America, this is so much superior in size to the rest that it bears to them the same proportion as a Golden Eagle to a Goshawk. It inhabits the valleys and plains of the western slope of the continent, and has not been observed to the eastward of the Rocky Mountains." - (Audubon's Ornithological Biography, 1831).

Fondation Beyeler Benefit Auction 2024 A benefit auction featuring works by artists such as Rirkrit Tiravanija, Dozie Kanu, Rudolf Stingel, Angela Bulloch, and Miriam Cahn. The auction includes a variety of art pieces, including paintings, sculptures, and installations, with details such as materials, dimensions, and edition numbers provided for each lot.

Dozie Kanu Dozie Kanu takes a hybrid approach that draws on both visual art and design. He refuses to define objects unambiguously. “I just can’t get bogged down by the concepts too much because it’s like soul spill.” as he himself once put it. For the summer exhibition at Fondation Beyeler (19 May–11 August 2024) Kanu pushed the boundaries of traditional sculpture and created objects that can be not just contemplated but also used. What he was striving for was a work that would leave the purely optical dimension behind and offer visitors a tactile experience as well. Inspired by Franz West’s chair installation at the Fondation Beyeler, Kanu invites visitors to take a seat on his chairs and to linger there for a while, even as the chairs themselves retain their status as works of art. Dozie Kanu (b. 1993) was born and raised in Houston, Texas, and later lived in New York before moving to Portugal. He is represented by the Galerie Francesca Pia in Zurich. The Studio Museum in Harlem, New York, staged a solo exhibition of his works in 2019 and there was another at the Kunstverein Essen, Germany, in 2022. Kanu won the “Catalogues for Young Artists Prize” awarded by the Alfried Krupp von Bohlen und Halbach-Stiftung in 2021, having previously won the Basel-based Hublot Design Prize in 2018. Aluminium, polyurethane foam. Colour: Nightblue and Purple. 71.3 × 79.6 × 23 cm. Edition: 1 + 1 AP. Copyright: Dozie Kanu. Courtesy: Dozie Kanu. Provenance: donated by the artist.

Rudolf Stingel Untitled, 2019 Celotex insulation board, wood, aluminum 115 × 115 × 4 cm Signed on the wooden panel on the reverse What can painting be? Rudolf Stingel constantly asks himself this question, and always rethinks it anew. Born in 1956 in Merano, Italy, in 1987 he moved to New York, where he still lives and works today. Since the start of his career in the late 1980s, he has been exploring artistic processes – often going beyond the traditional understanding of painting. He probes the possibilities of painting in the interplay of materials and textures. After his major solo shows in 2007 at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Chicago and the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York, he caused a sensation at the 2013 Venice Biennale, when he carpeted all the walls and floors of the Palazzo Grassi with Oriental rugs. “I am demonstrating that using different surfaces, we can produce very diverse environments.” Rudolf Stingel regularly intervenes in entire gallery and museum spaces. The present work is a fragment of the wall-to-wall, multi-room installation, constructed of Celotex insulation boards, which Stingel created for his major 2019 summer show at the Fondation Beyeler. On that occasion, he transformed parts of the exhibition, as well as the restaurant, into a silver-colored installation – and also into a palimpsest, into which visitors could literally inscribe themselves. Celotex insulation board, wood, aluminum 115 × 115 × 4 cm Signed on the wooden panel on the reverse Copyright: Rudolf Stingel Courtesy: Rudolf Stingel Photos: Mark Niedermann Provenance: donated by the artist

Rirkrit Tiravanija untitled 2024 (my body is filled with waiting / i wait for nothing, the japanese times, may 5, 1923), 2024 Acrylic gouache and newspaper on linen Each 60 × 80 cm The work of Rirkrit Tiravanija (b. 1961 Buenos Aires) has long been reverberating through the world of contemporary art. Focusing on the social role of the artist, he often invites viewers to inhabit, activate and participate in his work by cooking, eating, drinking and playing together. In the course of his 30-year-long career, he has made installations, painting, printmaking, video, photography, mixed-media assemblages and music an integral part of his practice. Newsprint connecting politics and the individual is an on-going aspect of Tiravanija’s art. He explores symbols in the collective consciousness, mines historical and contemporary narratives for imagery, examines bold aphorisms, allegories and political statements and interweaves the thematics of recognition with the dynamics of transmission. Rirkrit sourced the texts for the artwork presented at the benefit auction from the opera Hanjo by the Japanese composer Toshio Hosokawa, which was performed at Munich’s Haus der Kunst in 2023. He then printed them on Japanese dailies, prompting viewers to reconsider their interpretation of news across diverse languages and perspectives. Acrylic gouache and newspaper on linen Each 60 × 80 cm Copyright: Rirkrit Tiravanija Courtesy: Rirkrit Tiravanija Provenance: donated by the artist

Otto Piene Goldregen und Platinsoldaten, 2014 Gold, glaze on clay 63 × 63 × 3 cm #685 and #692 in the catalogue raisonné of Otto Piene’s ceramics Otto Piene (1928–2014) ranks among the pioneers of twentieth-century art. As a co-founder of the ZERO movement he joined Heinz Mack and Günther Uecker on their quest for an art unencumbered by the horrors of the Second World War. Piene created works that fundamentally transformed classical perceptions of sculpture and painting, most notably in his “grid pictures” his smoke- and fire-scorched paintings, his “light ballets” and, later, his “sky art”. His experiments with light, air and fire took him to MIT in Boston, where from the late 1960s onwards he was engaged in teaching and research at the interface of art and technology. Piene’s ceramics take the grid pictures and fire paintings to a new level. The textured surface of the glaze pressed onto them through a screen makes for an interesting play of light. The works are also defined by the arbitrariness of the firing process inside the kiln, which might easily have destroyed them. The two unique but affiliated ceramics that here are being auctioned off as a pair were created by Piene for an exhibition at the Nationalgalerie in Berlin in July 2014, shortly before his death. They therefore count among his last works. Gold, glaze on clay 63 × 63 × 3 cm #685 and #692 in the catalogue raisonné of Otto Piene’s ceramics Copyright: Otto Piene Trust Courtesy: Elizabeth Goldring Piene, Otto Piene Trust Provenance: donated by the Otto Piene Trust

Hélène Delprat Hélène Delprat (b. 1957 in Amiens) lives and works in Paris. She won a scholarship that enabled her to spend the years 1982 to 1984 at the Villa Medici in Rome. Over the past four decades, Delprat the painter, video artist, set designer and blogger has developed a wide-ranging, interdisciplinary body of work. She is represented by both the Galerie Christophe Gaillard and, since 2023, by Hauser & Wirth, whose Paris gallery staged a solo exhibition of her works in early 2024. Blast 3 belongs to a series of three works of 2019 that are typical of Delprat’s distinctive and painterly approach to art. The chaotic complexity of her paintings reflects the kind of layerings that are an essential aspect of several different artistic genres as well as many of her sources of inspiration, including comics, newspaper illustrations, feature films, Abstract Expressionism, English literature and Renaissance art – to name but a few. Blast 3 shows sketchy figures emerging from multiple layers of splintered splashes of paint, organic shapes, grids and lines, as if surfacing unbidden from the obscure depths of memory. Delprat is fascinated by paradoxes and contrasts and creates works in which the comical runs up against the repulsive and the figurative against the abstract. Her imaginary universe is adorned with pigments and gold sequins – and populated by fantastically deformed monsters. Pigments and acrylic on canvas 100 × 80 cm Signed and dated on the reverse H. Delprat 2019 Copyright: Hélène Delprat Provenance: donated by Marie-Puck Broodthaers

Urs Fischer For over twenty years, Urs Fischer (b. 1973 in Zurich) has created paintings, sculptures, and installations that disorient and bewilder, while exploring critical themes of perception and representation. A keen awareness of contemporary media and digitization forms a unifying thread across his remarkably diverse and deeply irreverent oeuvre. As curator Massimiliano Gioni suggests, “All of Fischer’s work... could be seen as an effort... to grant body and weight to the immateriality that seems to characterize the digital world.” Fischer’s fairytale-like bronze sculpture House on a Hill (2019), which reimagines the eponymous horror movie trope, is emblematic of this impulse. Usually an ominous castle or mansion, the cinematic “house on a hill” is typically residence to a villain or shadowy recluse and perceived as a site of danger. In a pointedly ironic twist, however, Fischer exaggerates the “hill” into a vertiginous spire, while playfully transforming the “house” into a quaint pink cottage. This parodical attempt to translate a filmic cliché to sculptural form – bringing a fantasy to life in a material context – both suggests the emptiness of Hollywood artifice and the persuasive magic of the film industry. Cast bronze, primer, gesso, oil paint, LED light, battery 55.9 × 15.2 × 14 cm AP 2/2 from an Edition of 2 + 2 AP Copyright: Urs Fischer Courtesy: Urs Fischer and Gagosian Photo: Mats Nordman Provenance: donated by the artist

Miriam Cahn Miriam Cahn (b. 1949 in Basel) creates art about human vulnerabilities and fearlessly faces big issues head on, be it war, violence, trauma, sexuality or death. Her treatment of taboo topics often evokes disquiet, which for her is a way of prompting society to look critically at its own norms. Her art, which is at once radical and personal, has made her one of Switzerland’s most successful contemporary artists. Just last year the Palais de Tokyo in Paris honoured her with a major retrospective. In Muttertraum Cahn seems to see the female body as a bearer of social meaning. The figure it depicts has duplicate maternal attributes (four breasts) and scarcely any face at all, since it portrays not an individual but the mother as concept, which viewers themselves can then imagine. By reducing the figure to her sexuality alone, Cahn deconstructs the social role of the mother. How can individuality and motherhood be reconciled? Works that grapple with personal matters are by no means atypical of this artist and it seems likely that here, too, social critique was not her only concern. Cahn herself never wanted to be a mother and Muttertraum shows her anatomising her perception of that role. Oil on canvas 140 × 66 cm Copyright: Miriam Cahn Provenance: donated by Eva Dichand Collection

A Regency Silver Plate Epergne A magnificent centerpiece, topped with a cut crystal bowl faceted with palmettes above a vasiform support cast with an anthemion band above wing spread swans and four scrolling candle arms supported on cast ram's heads joined by chain swags and a fluted support cast with perched eagles ending on a fluted spreading base with anthemion banding. 30in (76cm) h.22in (56cm) d. During the Regency, dining was often 'à la française,' meaning all dishes for a course were placed on the table simultaneously for guests to serve themselves. The epergne helped to elevate and organize the food, saving space on the often crowded tables.

3 Chinese Export Porcelain Armorial Serving Pieces Three Chinese Export Porcelain Armorial Serving Pieces, Circa. 1810, Comprising of two platters and a punch bowl, Platters and bowl are decorated with a sepia and gilt neoclassical band on the rim, surrounding a central shield with scrolled leaf and garland supports. Central initials JAD within the shield indicates these were made for the marriage of John and Ann Donnell of Baltimore, Maryland, the owners of Willowbrook Estate in Baltimore. The oval dining room from the Willowbrook mansion was removed and re-created in the Baltimore Museum of Art in the mid-1960's before the mansion was demolished. The dining table is set with this Chinese Export dinner service. A dinner plate from the service is in the Dietrich American Foundation. Literature: China for America: Export Porcelain of the 18th and 19th Centuries by Herbert F. Schiffer, Peter Berwind Schiffer, and Nancy Schiffer, p. 73. Largest platter. H: 2 x W: 13 1/2 x D: 11 in. The bowl has several cracks and restoration. General wear commensurate with use.

8 Rare Minton Porcelain Cabinet Plates Eight Rare Minton Porcelain Cabinet Plates, attributed to Christopher Dresser, 1877-1880. Belonging to Nelson A. Rockefeller, Vice President of the United States under Gerald Ford, these cabinet plates were designed during the Aesthetic Movement in the Japanese taste by renowned British designer Christopher Dresser. He was known to repeat motifs such as the distinctive floral design within a hexagon that is repeated in a geometric pattern on the green background. Each plate has a geometric border, stylized Greek keys on each side of the central white square, upon which are painted and gilded in yellow gold, rose gold and silver. H: 9 1/2 W: 9 1/2 D: 1 in. SPB Sale 4159, Lot 95. and PB Eighty-Four Sale 768 Lot 33. Exposition Universelle Paris of 1878. Minton Archive pattern books SD1705/MS2128 MS 2434 and SD1705/MS1508 confirm the design. Subjects depicted include songbirds, cherry blossom branches, floral and leaf decorations. These were designed to be cabinet plates to display, but the Rockefellers clearly used them as there is evidence of wear and fine knife scars. This is an assembled group, all produced by Minton in Stoke-upon-Trent, England, and many bear the stamp G3473, which means that they were among the most expensive, gilded plates that the Staffordshire firm produced. Two of these plates with birds also bear the gold Minton mark with Prince of Wales feathers. The Prince of Wales was the patron for the English companies participating in the Exposition Universalle in Paris 1878. They are dated 1877. The other six plates have letters for 1877 and 1880. The exposition plates also were sold in America by the New York retailer Gilman & Collamore. The Paris plates have a painted cursive S, which may refer to the gilder and others have four dots in a diamond shape or //. Christopher Dresser did not sign all of his designs for Minton, but according to the Minton Archive, he was known to repeat decorative motifs, which are seen here and on Minton porcelain designs documented to him.

SFO Museum Deaccessioning Project SFO Museum is conducting a review and downsizing of our holdings that fall outside our collection focus. In the museum profession, this process is called deaccessioning and is a regular part of responsible collections management. It ensures that the museum’s limited staff resources and storage space are allocated to objects that support and promote the core focus and purpose of the collection. Since the early 2000s, SFO Museum’s permanent collection has been defined and guided by the following statement: The collection focus of the San Francisco Airport Commission Library and Louis A. Turpen Aviation Museum (AML) is to collect objects and media that document the development, legacy, and cultural context of commercial aviation and the airline industry with an emphasis on the West Coast and Pacific Region, as well as the history and development of San Francisco International Airport (SFO). Since that time, the museum has collected objects, books and archival materials that are tightly focused on the history of commercial aviation and SFO. However, before this collection focus statement was crafted, the SFO Museum accessioned a wide variety of objects, both aviation-related and non-aviation-related alike, that do not fall within the bounds of our current collection focus. To meet our program objectives, SFO Museum has implemented a comprehensive review of its collection holdings acquired through purchase between the 1990s and early 2000s, identifying aviation and non-aviation-related objects that no longer support our collection focus. Objects acquired through donation are not part of this deaccessioning project. The deaccession candidates currently occupy 20% of available museum storage capacity while representing less than 1% of usage in our public programs. The deaccession process adheres to the highest professional standards in accordance with the American Alliance of Museums, San Francisco Airport Commission, and the City and County of San Francisco. Any revenues generated from the public sale of deaccessioned objects will be held in trust, designated for the future acquisition of objects for the San Francisco Airport Commission Library and Louis A. Turpen Aviation Museum. Through responsible collections stewardship, SFO Museum believes that this initiative will help to support our mission for many years to come.

SFO Museum Deaccessioning Project SFO Museum is conducting a review and downsizing of our holdings that fall outside our collection focus. In the museum profession, this process is called deaccessioning and is a regular part of responsible collections management. It ensures that the museum’s limited staff resources and storage space are allocated to objects that support and promote the core focus and purpose of the collection. Since the early 2000s, SFO Museum’s permanent collection has been defined and guided by the following statement: The collection focus of the San Francisco Airport Commission Library and Louis A. Turpen Aviation Museum (AML) is to collect objects and media that document the development, legacy, and cultural context of commercial aviation and the airline industry with an emphasis on the West Coast and Pacific Region, as well as the history and development of San Francisco International Airport (SFO). Since that time, the museum has collected objects, books and archival materials that are tightly focused on the history of commercial aviation and SFO. However, before this collection focus statement was crafted, the SFO Museum accessioned a wide variety of objects, both aviation-related and non-aviation-related alike, that do not fall within the bounds of our current collection focus. To meet our program objectives, SFO Museum has implemented a comprehensive review of its collection holdings acquired through purchase between the 1990s and early 2000s, identifying aviation and non-aviation-related objects that no longer support our collection focus. Objects acquired through donation are not part of this deaccessioning project. The deaccession candidates currently occupy 20% of available museum storage capacity while representing less than 1% of usage in our public programs. The deaccession process adheres to the highest professional standards in accordance with the American Alliance of Museums, San Francisco Airport Commission, and the City and County of San Francisco. Any revenues generated from the public sale of deaccessioned objects will be held in trust, designated for the future acquisition of objects for the San Francisco Airport Commission Library and Louis A. Turpen Aviation Museum. Through responsible collections stewardship, SFO Museum believes that this initiative will help to support our mission for many years to come.

Hilda Wilkinson Brown, 1894-1981, Oak Bluffs (Martha's Vineyard) Hilda Wilkinson Brown 1894-1981 Oak Bluffs (Martha's Vineyard) c. 1930 oil on canvas 16-1/8 x 24-5/8 inches initialed verso This extraordinary painting depicts the beach at Oak Bluffs, Massachusetts (Martha's Vineyard), known originally as The Inkwell, a historic African American summer resort, founded in the 1890s. That name was originally meant to be an insult issued by nearby white communities, referencing the skin color of beach-goers. Over time, that odious name became an emblem of pride and power to Black people. In the early 20th century, African Americans came to the islands to work as servants at the white-owned summer homes, but eventually, these people became modest property owners there, and began offering accommodations in their cottages to other Black vacationers. Black women played a major role in this transformation. Throughout the 20th century, Black enterprise in this area continued to grow, and the area became a center of great wealth. This work was featured in a documentary, Kindred Spirits, Artists Hilda Wilkinson Brown and Lilian Thomas Burwell, by filmmaker Cintia Cabib.

Aaron Douglas, 1899-1979, White Plains, New York Aaron Douglas 1899-1979 White Plains, New York 1940-1942 oil on canvas 25-3/4 x 27-1/2 inches signed label verso with White Plains, New York noted Provenance: Eva Lyons Sanders Sawyer to Dr Jane Prescott Brown, thence by descent to Christine Brown, Hazelcrest, IL. Eva Sawyer was born in Austin, TX to the well-known Carrington/Lyons family (Prescott Papers, Newberry Library, Chicago and the Austin History Center), and later moved to Queens, NY. She was connected socially and professionally to many of the Harlem Renaissance artists and likely acquired the painting from the artist during this time (originally, she owned three works by Douglas).

Elizabeth Catlett, 1915-2012, Olmec Bather #1 Elizabeth Catlett (1915-2012) created 'Olmec Bather #1' in 1966. The work is cast bronze, mounted on a wooden base, and stands 24 inches (h) tall, not including the base. It is initialed. The Olmec were an ancient Mesoamerican civilization that flourished along the Gulf Coast of Mexico from roughly 1500 to 300 BCE. Catlett executed several versions of Olmec Bather in the mid-1960s. Samella Lewis, in The Art of Elizabeth Catlett (1984-2000), pictures a similar version, Olmec Bather #2 (1967), which is listed as a little over 10' in height; this example, Olmec Bather #1 (listed on p. 188), and Olmec Bather #3 (in wood). Pictured in Elizabeth Catlett, A Black Revolutionary Artist and All That It Implies, the catalog accompanying the exhibition for the Brooklyn Museum and The National Gallery of Art (2024, p. 151) is Bañista Olmeca (Olmec Bather) her 120 inch tall commission for El Instituto Politécnico Nacional in Mexico City. That bronze is listed as being done in 1966. Melanie Herzog quotes the artist addressing this subject: "It is a tall woman with a big bust and a big behind and she's got a floating towel around her. I called it Olmec because it has Black features, and those were Black people in Mexico despite what many anthropologists say." This larger version of the standing figure has a different, more frontal pose with the arms resting at the sides.

Alfred Hair "Breaking Waves [Jumbo]" Oil on Upson Board Alfred Hair (Florida, 1941-1970) Breaking Waves Oil on Upson board 24" x 48" Signed to the lower right. The frame measures 27 1/4" x 51 1/4". Though Harold Newton is called the original Highwayman, the charismatic Alfred Hair is by all accounts the artist credited with the group's marketing model and collective vision as it was. Hair developed a talent for art in school, and in the 1950s he learned from the legendary Bean Backus, "the Dean of Florida Landscape Painters." With Backus's encouragement and training, and a traveling sales model he saw his friend Harold Newton employ, Hair found that there was an appetite for his landscapes. He quickly turned to painting full time. Throughout the 1960s, he painted at a breakneck pace, endlessly energetic, and he worked alongside friends he helped train as artists. This was the heyday of the group that would later be known as the Highwaymen. With his natural talent and years of training, Hair's work stands out compared to other Highwaymen as quick and expressionistic, and his style matched his desire to create enough work to make good money. He inspired his fellow Highwaymen, and was considered the beating heart of the group. His life ended tragically in 1970, murdered outside of a bar after an altercation. His death represented the traumatic end of a golden era for the Highwaymen. This single-owner Florida Highwaymen collection was thoughtfully assembled between 1998 and 2004, with works acquired from respected sources such as the A. E. Backus Gallery and Museum, Florida art dealers John Phillips and Bob Lablanc, and noted collectors Ty and Jean Tyson.

George Buckner "Clouds over Florida Waters" Oil on Board George Buckner (Florida, 1942-2002) Clouds over Florida Waters Oil on board 24" x 36" Signed to the bottom right corner. The frame measures 27 1/2" x 39 1/2". George Buckner, a native of Gifford, Florida, was the oldest of 12 children. George's father passed away when George was in high school, leading him to drop out of school and help his mother financially support the large family. After encountering Harold Newton, Buckner realized that there was money to be made in creating landscapes, prompting him and his brother Ellis to join the Highwaymen. He studied painting with Newton, Livingston Roberts, and Bean Backus, growing to become known as one of the strongest artists among the Highwaymen, particularly noted for his cloudy skies. This single-owner Florida Highwaymen collection was thoughtfully assembled between 1998 and 2004, with works acquired from respected sources such as the A. E. Backus Gallery and Museum, Florida art dealers John Phillips and Bob Lablanc, and noted collectors Ty and Jean Tyson. Very good condition. A few small dings to the top left corner. No in-painting or over-painting noted under blacklight inspection.

Harold Newton "Approaching Storm [Jumbo]" Oil on Upson Board Harold Newton (Florida, 1934-1994) Approaching Storm Oil on Upson board 32" x 48" Signed to the bottom right. The frame measures 37 1/2" x 53 1/2". Harold Newton is considered the first, most important, and most talented of all of the Highwaymen. Born one of 18 children in a blended Florida family, Newton was raised in Georgia, and moved out on his own and back to Florida's Treasure Coast as a teenager. He had always been interested in art, and a 1954 meeting with Bean Backus, the Dean of Florida Landscape Painters, profoundly shaped his own work and career to come. Newton watched Backus work in his studio for long stretches, gaining an arsenal of techniques, and when Newton artfully replicated Backus's scenery, he was met with wonder and great encouragement. In 1954, Newton began to peddle his own landscapes on the street, the only sales model available to African Americans in the Jim Crow South. He found enough success to inspire his friend Alfred Hair, a student of Backus, to follow his lead selling door-to-door. Newton and Hair inspired numerous other aspiring artists to learn their styles and techniques, and one by one, a loose group of 26 variously connected Black artists came together to form a collective of sorts that would retrospectively be known as the Highwaymen. Though the more social Alfred Hair was considered the heart of the group, it was Harold Newton's skillful painting that inspired most of all. Highwayman James Gibson said "If it wasn't for Harold, we wouldn't really have been painting. He could paint better than we could." Willie Daniels, another Highwayman, commented, "Harold was number one. Everyone wanted to be like him. Masterpieces off the top of his head." And Highwayman Roy McLendon considered Newton to be every bit as good as Bean Backus, and "sometimes better." Harold Newton remained devoted to his art throughout his life, before he tragically passed away at 59 years old, right before a major critical reassessment and interest in the Highwaymen began in earnest. Literature: Gary Monroe, Harold Newton: The Original Highwayman, University Press of Florida, Gainesville, 2007. This single-owner Florida Highwaymen collection was thoughtfully assembled between 1998 and 2004, with works acquired from respected sources such as the A. E. Backus Gallery and Museum, Florida art dealers John Phillips and Bob Lablanc, and noted collectors Ty and Jean Tyson.

In 1954, Newton began to peddle his own landscapes on the street, the only sales model available to African Americans in the Jim Crow South. He found enough success to inspire his friend Alfred Hair, a student of Backus, to follow his lead selling door-to-door. Newton and Hair inspired numerous other aspiring artists to learn their styles and techniques, and one by one, a loose group of 26 variously connected Black artists came together to form a collective of sorts that would retrospectively be known as the Highwaymen. Though the more social Alfred Hair was considered the heart of the group, it was Harold Newton's skillful painting that inspired most of all. Highwayman James Gibson said "If it wasn't for Harold, we wouldn't really have been painting. He could paint better than we could." Willie Daniels, another Highwayman, commented, "Harold was number one. Everyone wanted to be like him. Masterpieces off the top of his head." And Highwayman Roy McLendon considered Newton to be every bit as good as Bean Backus, and "sometimes better." Harold Newton remained devoted to his art throughout his life, before he tragically passed away at 59 years old, right before a major critical reassessment and interest in the Highwaymen began in earnest. Literature: Gary Monroe, Harold Newton: The Original Highwayman, University Press of Florida, Gainesville, 2007. This single-owner Florida Highwaymen collection was thoughtfully assembled between 1998 and 2004, with works acquired from respected sources such as the A. E. Backus Gallery and Museum, Florida art dealers John Phillips and Bob Lablanc, and noted collectors Ty and Jean Tyson.

Livingston Roberts "Everglades Sunset" Oil on Upson Board Livingston Roberts (Florida, 1941-2004) Everglades Sunset Oil on Upson board 24" x 36" Signed to the lower right. The frame measures 27 1/2" x 39 1/2". Livingston Roberts was the first artist to join Harold Newton and Alfred Hair painting and selling Florida landscapes. Roberts began to paint as a teenager and developed by watching Bean Backus work -- it was at Backus's studio that Roberts met Hair. Revered among his peers as a mentor and inspiration, fellow Highwayman Al Black called Livingston Roberts "the best painter of all the Highwaymen, and the nicest." This single-owner Florida Highwaymen collection was thoughtfully assembled between 1998 and 2004, with works acquired from respected sources such as the A. E. Backus Gallery and Museum, Florida art dealers John Phillips and Bob Lablanc, and noted collectors Ty and Jean Tyson. Condition: Good condition. There is a small scuff above the horizon along the right edge, and a few frame abrasions along the extreme edges. No in-painting or over-painting noted upon blacklight inspection.

Herend Hand Painted Porcelain Rust Fishnet Joyful Elephant Figurine A hand painted porcelain figurine manufactured by Herend of Hungary that depicts a joyful elephant standing upright with its trunk raised, a posture often associated with good fortune. The body is decorated in a characteristic rust-red fishnet pattern over a white ground. The ears are finished in a light blue/celadon glaze. The tusks and edges of the feet are accented with gold luster detailing. The underside bears the official blue stamped mark reading "HEREND HUNGARY HANDPAINTED" with the handwritten model numbers "362" on the base. Dimensions: 3.50"L x 2.50"W x 3.75"H. Weight: 2.30oz.

Two Nils Landberg Orrefors Sommerso Dusk Vases This lot consists of two pieces of heavy Mid-Century Modern art glass designed by Nils Landberg for Orrefors, Sweden, consistent with the Sommerso 'Dusk' series. The primary item is a tall, rectangular columnar vase executed in thick-walled olive-green tinted glass encased in clear glass. The underside of this vessel is etched with the inscription "Orrefors R 3736." The second item is a smaller, footed vase featuring a conical body rendered in smoky gray glass with a clear stem and a distinct, asymmetrical scalloped rim. Both pieces exhibit the heavy, layered glass technique characteristic of the period. Dimensions: 2.50"W x 9.00"H

Hand Carved Natural Banded Jasper Bear Figurine with Red Agate Fish A hand carved natural stone bear figurine executed in a highly polished banded stone, consistent with Kalahari Jasper. The stone displays distinct swirling layers of cream, beige, and reddish-brown tones. The stylized quadruped is depicted in a hunched, feeding posture, holding a small, separate object in its mouth. This object is carved from translucent, deep reddish-brown stone, likely red agate or carnelian, shaped to resemble a fish or salmon. This type of animal carving is often marketed as a fetish from the Zuni culture. Dimensions: 2.50"L x 1.50"W x 1.50"H. Weight: 2.60oz.

(2) Qiu Deshu (1948-2020) Chinese, Abstract Fissured Paper Collage Qiu Deshu (1948-2020) Chinese, Abstract Fissured Paper Collage. Pair of abstract 'fissured' collages of torn and reapplied paper, creating a pair of kinetic, unique compositions. Signed lower right. Framed. Provenance: Acquired by Ethan Cohen who bought the artwork directly from the artist at his home in China. Overall Size: 17 x 10 in. Sight Size: 16 x 9 in. Qiu Deshu (Born 1948, Shanghai, China) is a contemporary Chinese painter and printmaker renowned for his innovative contributions to modern ink painting. A graduate of the Shanghai Academy of Fine Arts, Qiu emerged as a leading figure in the “No Name Group,” an avant-garde collective that challenged conventional Chinese artistic traditions in the 1970s. He is best known for his “Fissure” series, which combines abstract ink textures with sculptural, layered surfaces, exploring the tension between chance, control, and natural forms. Qiu’s work bridges traditional Chinese materials and techniques with experimental, contemporary approaches, earning him international recognition through exhibitions in Asia, Europe, and the United States, and securing his place as a pivotal figure in China’s post–Cultural Revolution art scene.

(8) Circle of Qi Baishi (1864-1957) Chinese, Ink and Watercolor Paintings (8) Circle of Qi Baishi (1864-1957) Chinese, Ink and Watercolor Paintings. Group of eight in and watercolor paintings accompanied by calligraphic verse. The paintings were obtained in China from Qi Baishi himself. The works were attributed to his students, but there is a possibility that the calligraphy and seals were done by the hand of the artist himself. Size: 10 3/4 x 13 3/4 in. Qi Baishi (1864-1957) was a renowned Chinese painter and calligrapher, celebrated for his expressive and vibrant depictions of nature, including flowers, insects, birds, and landscapes. Born in rural Hunan, Qi initially worked as a carpenter before turning to art, studying traditional Chinese painting techniques while developing his own distinctive style. His works, often imbued with simplicity and spontaneity, combine elements of traditional Chinese brushwork with a more personal, emotive approach. Qi's use of bold brushstrokes and vivid colors gained him widespread recognition, and he is regarded as one of the most important Chinese artists of the 20th century. His legacy endures, with his works held in prominent museums worldwide.

Antique Russian Orthodox Partial Gilt Icon of Mary and the Christ Child Antique Russian Orthodox Partial Gilt Icon of Mary and the Christ Child. In 2004, Icon specialists Wendy Salmond and Ingrid Zoetmulder studied this work, valuing it at 5,000-6,000 Euros. This design is based on the Virgin of Tikhvin and bears Cyrillic writing dating the painting to April 10, 1868. The silver cover was made in Moscow with marks 84, ON, NK, & O, maker's symbols from 1875. Overall Size: 14 1/4 x 13 3/4 in. Sight Size: 6 1/4 x 5 1/4 in. Russian icons are sacred religious images, traditionally painted on wood panels and gilded with gold leaf, that serve as focal points for Orthodox Christian worship and devotion. Emerging in the medieval period and heavily influenced by Byzantine art, icons depict Christ, the Virgin Mary, saints, and biblical scenes with highly stylized, symbolic forms, emphasizing spiritual presence over naturalistic representation. Artists adhered to strict canonical guidelines for composition, color, and gesture, aiming to convey divine truth and facilitate prayer rather than personal expression. Beyond their religious function, Russian icons are admired for their luminous surfaces, intricate detailing, and profound sense of stillness and transcendence, making them both devotional objects and enduring masterpieces of spiritual artistry.

Charles J. Bridgman (1841-1895) American, Exceptional Oil on Canvas "Battledore & Shuttlecock (1873)" Among Charles Bridgman’s known works, the offered painting stands out as one of his finest. The figures’ costumes suggest a Brittany cottage, richly detailed with the accoutrements typical of a mid‑19th-century studio. At the center, a mother and child play Battledore and Shuttlecock, an early form of modern badminton. The studio is densely populated with carefully arranged props that take on a still-life quality, reflecting the artist’s personality and meticulous eye. In the lower left, a copy of Danish composer Friedrich Kuhlau’s Three Fantasies for Flute Solo lies on the floor, partially hidden behind a flute propped against the easel—Bridgman himself was reportedly an amateur flautist. The easel holds a sweeping dusk landscape and is adorned with embroidered fabric, silk ribbons, and fresh flowers. On the upper-left wall, an evergreen branch hangs above a religious print of the Virgin and Child. To the right, two portraits of Brittany-costumed girls hang alongside a small depiction of a thatched-roof village, complete with a flower garland. The walls also feature a plaster cast of a face and a bird’s nest. Chairs in both the foreground and right corner are draped with textiles, with the foreground chair further decorated by a Venetian-style Mardi Gras mask. In the lower right, near a feathered shuttlecock, sits an artist’s palette loaded with fresh paint. Suspended at the upper margin, a large mercury-reflective bulb hangs from a blue silk ribbon, capturing a mirrored view of the opposite studio wall, including a window and a gallery of paintings. Signed lower right. Signed and titled verso. Overall Size: 30 1/4 x 26 1/4 in. Sight Size: 21 1/2 x 17 1/2 in. Charles J. Bridgman (1841–1895) was an American painter active in the mid-to-late 19th century, known for his figure and genre scenes, as well as occasional landscapes and marine subjects. Born in Tuskegee, Alabama, he was part of a family connected to the arts — his brother was the more famous orientalist painter Frederick Arthur Bridgman. Charles spent part of his career working in France as well as in New Orleans, Louisiana, reflecting transatlantic influences in his work. His drawings and paintings occasionally surfaced among collections — for example, the national collections in the United States include some of his works — but he never achieved the notoriety of his brother. Bridgman’s output remains relatively obscure today; what survives suggests an artist comfortable with narrative tableau and traditional academic techniques, working in a period rich with genre painting and shifting artistic tastes.

Frank Harmon Myers (1899-1956) American, Farm Scene Oil on Board Frank Harmon Myers (1899-1956) American, Farm Scene Oil on Board. Signed bottom right. Written on the back of the board: "Mr. Gamble 'Sunlight + Glade' Sketch." Overall Size: 12 1/2 x 14 1/2 in. Sight Size: 9 1/2 x 11 1/2 in. Frank Harmon Myers was born in Cleves, Ohio in 1899 and studied at the Art Academy of Cincinnati before traveling to France to paint alongside John Ellsworth Weis in 1921. He returned to Cincinnati in 1925 to teach at the Art Academy and married a fellow teacher, Ella Price, traveling extensively before winding up in Santa Fe, New Mexico in 1932 to paint with Joseph Henry Sharp. After moving to Pacific Grove, California his Impressionist seascapes brought him international acclaim, and he served as president of the Carmel Art Association in 1953, widely exhibiting his works around the world and having his pieces added to the permanent collections of the National Museum of American Art, the San Diego Art Museum, and the Smithsonian Institute, among others. He died of a heart attack in San Francisco on March 7th, 1956, and his brightly colored expressive brush strokes and impact of French impressionism continued to have a lasting influence on artists in the area for decades afterwards.

Frank Myers (1899-1956) American, Oil on Canvas, "Valley Ranch, New Mexico" Frank Myers (1899-1956) American, Oil on Canvas, "Valley Ranch, New Mexico". Overall Size: 31 x 36 in. Sight Size: 24 1/2 x 29 1/2 in. Ohio born Frank H. Myers enrolled at the Cincinnati Art Academy in 1917, studying with Frank Duveneck (1848-1919) and John E. Weis (1892-1962), and later, at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts. In 1925, Myers married Ella Price, a young school teacher. They spent their honeymoon in Europe, staying in Paris and taking trips throughout Spain. The following year, they made an extended trip west, to Colorado and California, and on their return, spent several weeks in Santa Fe and Taos, where he met Joseph Henry Sharp (1859-1953). Myers's work includes portraits as well as landscapes and urban scenes. His early paintings show a strong sense of realism handled in a bold and expressive brush stroke, and at other times, he produced brightly colored works showing his keen interest in French Impressionism. Gradually, he developed a strong sense of abstract design and in the late 1920s, he produced a number remarkably advanced paintings in an analytical style bordering on abstraction. In the late 1930s, Myers's interest increasingly turned to painting the ocean. At the same time, his health began to falter and for no apparent reason, he experienced bouts of depression. In 1940, he took what was to be a year's leave of absence and moved to Monterey. Captivated by the beauty of the California coast, he remained the rest of his life. Thereafter, his work was almost exclusively seascapes, with an occasional portrait commission. A well-respected leader of the Carmel-Monterey art community, Myers served as president of the Carmel Art Association, in 1953. His painting companions included Armin C. Hansen (1886-1957) and Donald Teague (born 1897), and together they defined the Monterey art scene for many years. After several years of recurring health problems, Frank Myers died of a heart attack on March 7, 1956. Myers paintings are in the National Museum of American Art in Washington D. C., the Museum of New Mexico, the University of Cincinnati, and at the Irvine Museum.

Giuseppe Carelli (1858-1921) Italian, Oil on Canvas, "The Procession" Giuseppe Carelli (1858-1921) Italian, Oil on Canvas, "The Procession". Signed lower right. Overall Size: 16 3/4 x 31 in. Sight Size: 11 3/4 x 26 1/4 in. Giuseppe Carelli (1858 - 1921) was active/lived in Italy. Giuseppe Carelli is known for Landscape and marine painting. Giuseppe Carelli was born in Naples in 1858 into a family of Neapolitan artists - his grandfather Raffaele Carelli (1795 – 1864 ), father Consalvo Carelli (1818 – 1900), and uncles Gabriele and Achille Carelli were all successful artists belonging to the School of Posillipo. This group of artists was influenced by the beauty of Naples and the surrounding coastline and villages, producing lyrical landscapes in both oils and watercolors. Giuseppe Carelli was initially taught by his father, Consalvo, and by the age of eighteen he graduated from the Institute of Fine Arts in Naples, where he studied with Joseph Mancinelli and Vincenzo Marinelli, before continuing his studies in Rome.

Henry Strater (1896-1987) American, Oil on Canvas, "Titian Eve" Henry Strater (1896-1987) American, Oil on Canvas, "Titian Eve". 1970 signed and dated lower left. Titled and inscribed Henry Strater Ogunquit, Maine on the back. Overall Size: 35 1/2 x 23 1/2 in. Sight Size: 29 1/2 x 17 1/2 in. Provenance: Peter Klipstas. Portola Valley, California. Purchased from Frank Rehn Gallery, New York City, in 1971. Henry Strater is probably best known for his portraits of his friend, Ernest Hemingway. He also illustrated first editions of works by Ezra Pound and Archibald MacLeish. He counted F. Scott Fitzgerald among his friends from his Princeton University days. He served as an ambulance driver in World War I, staying in France after the war to study at the Academie Julien. He studied at the Art Students League in New York upon his return from Europe and then at an art school in Ogunquit, Maine, where he garnered the nickname, "Rock-a-Day Strater." He maintained a studio in Ogunquit for 55 years and served as president of the local art association. He also had a studio in New York.

Important Meiji Period Japanese Imperial Gilded Fabric Four Panel Folding Screen Room Divider Depicting Itsukushima Shrine Important Meiji Period Japanese Imperial Gilded Fabric Four Panel Folding Screen Room Divider Depicting Itsukushima Shrine. This four-panel Japanese screen depicts the Itsukushima Shrine nestled within a coastal landscape, featuring the iconic "floating" torii gate and various boats navigating the deep blue waters. This four panel screen comes in 2 parts. The composition of one of them is characterized by traditional gold leaf clouds that frame the scene, separating the detailed architectural structures from the distant, rolling green mountains. The second one features groups of people gathered on red mats along the shoreline, while several small boats with white sails navigate the deep blue waters of the bay. Size: 67 1/4 x 73 3/4 in (each 2 panel screen). Itsukushima Shrine is a Shinto sanctuary located on the island of Miyajima in Hiroshima Bay, renowned for its iconic vermilion torii gate that appears to float on the sea at high tide. Originally established in the 6th century and later expanded under the patronage of the warlord Taira no Kiyomori, the shrine is dedicated to the three Munakata goddesses associated with the sea, storms, and safe passage. Built on stilts over tidal flats, its architecture harmonizes with the surrounding landscape, reflecting Shinto principles of purity and reverence for nature by minimizing disturbance to the sacred island itself. The shrine’s shifting relationship with the tides underscores themes of impermanence and balance, making Itsukushima both a spiritual site and a living expression of Japan’s integration of ritual, environment, and aesthetics.

Keith Ward (1906-2000) American, Canyon Lands Keith Ward (1906-2000) American, Canyon Lands. Signed and dated 1981. Landscape near Taos Pueblo in winter. Overall Size: 37 1/2 x 41 7/8 in. Sight Size: 36 1/4 x 39 1/2 in. Born in Iola, KS on Aug. 7, 1906. Ward moved to Modesto, CA when quite young. Upon graduation from high school, he was awarded a scholarship to the CSFA where he was a pupil of Spencer Macky. His art studies were continued at the AIC and Grand Central School of Art in NYC. Most of his early career was spent in the East where he was a successful illustrator of children's books and national magazines such as Life, Saturday Evening Post, and Colliers for which he won many awards. He was also the creator of Elsie the Borden Cow and other well-known logos. Ward lived and taught in Palm Springs, CA and was active as an artist into the 1990s. He died in Georgetown, CA on April 20, 2000. An Impressionist, his work includes landscapes still lifes, marines, and portraits. Member: SWA; Silvermine (CT) Guild; Desert Art Center (Palm Springs).

Large Antique Gilt Wood Burmese Mandalay Standing Buddha Statue - AS IS Large Antique Gilt Wood Burmese Mandalay Standing Buddha Statue - AS IS. Bejeweled with some orange hue showing through where the gilt has rubbed away, standing on a black wooden stand. Size: 22 x 9 1/2 x 57 1/2 in. The position of the right hand slightly raised is called the Abhaya mudra. The term Abhaya in Sanskrit means "fearlessness," and this gesture symbolizes protection, peace, benevolence, and the dispelling of fear. The left hand in the statue is held down, which is typically the Varada mudra. This gesture represents charity, compassion, and the granting of boons or wishes. When displayed together in a standing posture, the combination of the Abhaya and Varada mudras signifies the Buddha's promise to both offer divine protection and grant the wishes or blessings of his followers. Naoto Nakagawa (Born 1944, Kobe, Japan) immigrated to New York City in 1962 and has exhibited widely in both the United States and Japan. His early shows included the avant-garde Judson Gallery in New York (1968) and later exhibitions at Obelisk Gallery in Boston, OK Harris, Allan Frumkin, Victoria Munroe, and Feature Inc., as well as Fuji Television Gallery and Tamada Projects in Tokyo. A two-part survey of his work was recently held at White Box and Ethan Cohen Fine Arts, with essays by Alexandra Munroe, Eric Shiner, John Perreault, and Jonathan Goodman. Nakagawa’s paintings have also appeared in group exhibitions at major institutions such as the Guggenheim Museum, the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, and the Venice Biennale (2005). His work is represented in prominent collections, including the Museum of Modern Art in New York, MIT, the Worcester Museum, and Japan’s National Museums of Modern Art in Osaka and Kyoto. A National Endowment for the Arts grant recipient, Nakagawa has taught at Columbia University and Parsons School of Design and lectured at leading museums across the U.S.

Dan McCaw (b. 1942) 30 x 40 inches Artist: Dan McCaw Title: A Warning Medium: Oil on board Dimensions: 30 x 40 inches Signed: Signed lower right Framed/Base: 41 x 51 inches. Visualize yourself a mountain man in the early 1800's riding across the high plains with your partner, seeing the open landscape over a distance of many miles, sensing that you might be first human to ride across this vast area, sensing no danger whatsoever. You come upon this assembly of a feathered shaft embedded in the ground surrounded by a carefully placed ring of rocks. Is this a warning? The painting depicts two riders, one of which is obviously the bold leader, wearing a bright vermillion coat and no hat whose horse is attentive to the surrounding land. The other rider is clearly the follower, dressed in much more subdued colors, riding a horse whose head is down, paying little attention to any potential danger. The two riders are travelling through sage-covered land whose open vistas seem tranquil, suggesting no apparent hidden dangers. To come across this surprising shrine quickly sharpens their curiosity as to what it means, who erected it, and are its creators hidden behind the seemingly benign sagebrush that stretches on for miles in all directions.

The Antiquarians: The Collection of Susan and Alan Miller - January 28, 2026 Susan and Alan Miller were discerning antiquarians whose refined eye and deep intellectual curiosity shaped a lifetime of thoughtful collecting. Susan’s passion for textiles was a key pillar of their collecting, guided by a fascination with the history embedded in woven patterns, embroidered motifs, and the centuries-old design traditions they reflect. This connection to historical references extended across the couple’s collection, where highlights include European drawings, Asian works of art, and European and American furniture.

DE GRISOGONO, SWITZERLAND, REF. S09, ALLEGRA WHITE GOLD AND DIAMOND A fine lady's 18k white gold rectangular curved quartz wristwatch with white dial, pink sapphires and diamonds set bezel. EBERHARD & CO., SWITZERLAND, OVERSIZE CHRONOGRAPH STAINLESS STEEL A fine and rare and oversized stainless steel manual winding chronograph wristwatch with silver dial and two registers. Renowned for its precision chronographs since the early 20th century, Eberhard produced some of the most elegant and mechanically sophisticated timepieces of the interwar and postwar periods. The present example, notable for its large stainless steel case, embodies the brand’s pursuit of both functionality and refinement. Its silver dial, featuring two perfectly balanced registers and blued steel hands, offers exceptional legibility and timeless appeal. The oversized proportions, remarkable for its era, give the watch a commanding presence on the wrist, while retaining the subtle charm of classic chronograph design. A rare and desirable piece, this Eberhard “Oversize Chronograph” stands as a testament to the golden age of Swiss watchmaking where precision engineering met enduring elegance.

GAGA MILANO, SWITZERLAND, REF. 5096WG, MANUALE THIN, 18K WHITE GOLD Playful yet opulent, the Manuale Thin captures Gaga Milano’s distinctive vision — where traditional craftsmanship meets bold, modern expression. The present example, executed in luxurious 18K white gold and lavishly adorned with diamonds, embodies the maison’s flair for exuberant design and refined detail. Its deep black dial, animated by a vivid array of multicolored numerals, evokes a joyful sense of movement and individuality, while the slim profile of the case lends the piece an unexpected elegance. Beneath its whimsical charm lies Swiss precision, ensuring reliable timekeeping wrapped in unmistakable style. A rare and striking creation, this diamond-set Manuale Thin reflects Gaga Milano’s artistic spirit, a watch that transforms time into an expression of color, luxury, and personality.

GUBELIN, SWITZERLAND, "CIOCCOLATONE " IPSOMATIC YELLOW GOLD A fine and very rare square 18k yellow gold self-winding wristwatch with silver dial, diamonds indexes and center second. Renowned for its impeccable craftsmanship and refined design, Gübelin has long stood at the crossroads of haute horlogerie and jewelry artistry. The present “Cioccolatone” named affectionately for its soft, square case reminiscent of a fine chocolate praline exemplifies the mid-century golden age of Swiss design. Executed in warm yellow gold, the watch features a beautifully balanced silver dial adorned with diamond-set indexes, offering a discreet touch of glamour. Its self-winding “Ipsomatic” movement reflects Gübelin’s commitment to technical innovation and horological precision. Graceful yet substantial on the wrist, this rare “Cioccolatone Ipsomatic” captures the elegance of 1950s Swiss watchmaking, an era when proportion, craftsmanship, and subtle luxury defined true distinction.

HAMILTON, SWITZERLAND, BOMB TIMER WATCH, STAINLESS STEEL A very fine and rare, stainless steel wristwatch with black dial, rectangular shaped case. Developed during the Second World War, the Hamilton “Bomb Timer” represents one of the most fascinating chapters in military horology. Designed for use by Allied aircrews, these robust timing units were mounted within aircraft cockpit panels or bomb-release mechanisms to ensure absolute precision during aerial missions. The present example, housed in a solid stainless steel case with large screw-mounted lugs, exemplifies the rugged engineering and functional clarity demanded by wartime service. Its striking black dial with luminous Arabic numerals and subsidiary seconds offers superb legibility under low-light conditions. Far more than a conventional wristwatch, this Hamilton “Bomb Timer” is a tangible artifact of aviation history, a tool born from necessity, embodying both the ingenuity and courage of a defining era.

HEUER, SWITZERLAND, REF. 2446, AUTAVIA "JOCHEN RINDT" STAINLESS STEEL A fine and rare stainless steel manual winding pilot chronograph wristwatch with revolving bezel, black dial and 3 registers. Produced in the mid-1960s, the Reference 2446 “Autavia” is among Heuer’s most iconic creations, a cornerstone of the brand’s golden age of motorsport chronographs. The present example belongs to the celebrated “Jochen Rindt” configuration, named after the legendary Formula 1 World Champion who famously wore this model, forever linking it to the adrenaline-fueled world of racing. Its matte black dial with white subdials, luminous indexes, and large dauphine hands delivers superb legibility, while the rotating bezel adds a layer of rugged functionality. Housed in a robust stainless steel case, it combines tool-watch practicality with mid-century design purity. Coveted by collectors for its historical significance and balanced aesthetics, the Autavia Ref. 2446 “Jochen Rindt” stands as one of the most enduring expressions of Heuer’s racing heritage, a true icon of speed, precision, and timeless style.

HEUER, SWITZERLAND, REF. 2447, PRE CARRERA, STAINLESS STEEL A very fine and rare, stainless steel, manual wind wristwatch with gilt dial, radium hands. Predating the legendary Carrera, the Reference 2447 represents the genesis of Heuer’s dedication to precision chronographs. Produced in the 1950s, this model embodies the brand’s transition from utilitarian timing instruments to refined wristwatches designed for professional use and everyday wear alike. The present example features a beautifully balanced dial layout with applied indexes, subsidiary registers, and original radium-filled hands, now aged to a warm, green hue that testifies to the passage of time. Its robust stainless steel case captures the functional elegance that would later define the Carrera line. A piece of early Heuer history, the Ref. 2447 “Pre-Carrera” remains a cornerstone for collectors, a chronograph that bridges the tool-watch tradition of the brand’s early years with the design purity of its golden era.

JAEGER-LECOULTRE, SWITZERLAND, REF. ATMOS, ATMOS, BRASS An a very fine and rare, brass and crystal, self winding table clock. Few creations capture the poetry of mechanical ingenuity as completely as Jaeger-LeCoultre’s Atmos clock. Invented in 1928 and perfected by the maison in the decades that followed, the Atmos is powered not by winding or electricity, but by the natural variations of temperature and air pressure — transforming subtle atmospheric shifts into the quiet rhythm of time. Encased in luminous crystal panels and framed by polished brass, the movement appears to float in serene equilibrium. The balance of geometry, transparency, and precision embodies Jaeger-LeCoultre’s unique mastery of both engineering and artistry. An enduring icon of twentieth-century design, the Atmos stands not merely as a timekeeper, but as a living sculpture — an object where science and beauty exist in perfect harmony.

JAEGER LECOULTRE, SWITZERLAND, REF. E861, MEMOVOX GT STAINLESS STEEL A fine and rare stainless steel tonneau shaped self-winding alarm wristwatch with silver dial and center second. Introduced in the late 1960s, the Ref. E861 “Memovox GT”, short for Grand Touring stands as one of the most distinctive expressions of Jaeger-LeCoultre’s famed alarm wristwatch line. Combining sleek sport-inspired design with mechanical ingenuity, it captures the spirit of an era defined by modernity and motion. The present example features a brushed silver dial with contrasting black chapter ring, applied indexes, and two crowns for setting the time and alarm function. Its tonneau-shaped case, both dynamic and elegant, reflects the aesthetic experimentation that marked the brand’s designs of the period. Powered by the automatic calibre 916, the first high-frequency alarm movement by Jaeger-LeCoultre, the Memovox GT epitomizes the maison’s mastery of technical innovation and style, a true icon of functional sophistication.

JAEGER LECOULTRE, SWITZERLAND, REVERSO, STAINLESS STEEL AND YELLOW GOLD A very fine and elegant, stainless steel and yellow gold, manual wind wristwatch, two tone dial, revolving case. Originally conceived in 1931 for British polo players stationed in India, the Reverso remains one of the most iconic designs in horological history. Its ingenious swiveling case, created to protect the fragile glass from impact, transformed a sporting necessity into a masterpiece of Art Deco design. The present example, combining stainless steel and yellow gold, epitomizes the refined duality at the heart of the Reverso concept: elegance and function in perfect balance. The aged two-tone dial, with its Arabic numerals and chemin-de-fer minute track, radiates vintage charm and authenticity, enhanced by the warmth of its gilt details. A timeless emblem of ingenuity and style, this early mixed-metal Reverso embodies Jaeger-LeCoultre’s enduring pursuit of technical artistry and aesthetic grace, a true icon of 20th-century design.

LANCO, SWITZERLAND, REF. 237, BARRACUDA, STAINLESS STEEL Evoking the adventurous spirit of mid-century dive watches, the Lanco “Barracuda” stands as a testament to the brand’s commitment to robust design and functional beauty during the golden age of Swiss tool watches. Its sleek stainless steel case, paired with a striking matte black dial, embodies the purposeful elegance that defined the 1960s era of exploration. The luminous hands and indexes ensure legibility under all conditions, while the automatic movement within speaks to Lanco’s pursuit of precision and reliability. The bold “Barracuda” designation hints at both the aquatic inspiration and the rugged character for which this model is celebrated. A charming survivor from a time when form followed function, the present Ref. 237 remains a symbol of classic utilitarian design, a modest yet evocative piece of Swiss horological history.

OMEGA, SWITZERLAND, REF. 275.00.32, SPEEDMASTER, MOTHER-OF-PEARL DIAL, DIAMOND BEZEL, STAINLESS STEEL Representing a refined and contemporary interpretation of Omega’s iconic Speedmaster, the present Ref. 275.00.32 harmonizes sportiness with jewelry-like elegance. Anne Packard (1933- ) Provincetown Harbor, Oil on panel, 5 x 7", 7 1/4 x 9 1/4" framed Anne Packard (1933- ) Provincetown Harbor, Oil on panel, 5 x 7", 7 1/4 x 9 1/4" framed. Dimensions: 5 x 7", 7 1/4 x 9 1/4" framed. Medium: Oil on panel. Notes: Signed LR. Condition: Very good condition. Anne Packard was born in 1933 in Hyde Park, New Jersey and spent her summers in Provincetown, Massachusetts where she moved in 1977, after raising five children. Her early work was painting on wood panels and weathered shingles, but mediums now include oil, monotypes and giclee. She attended Bard College, and studied with the late Phil Malicoat. Packard has traveled extensively, and locations include the Outer Cape, Ireland, Italy, Greece and Mexico. Selected publications, Art Business News, Associated Press, Channel 7, Boston, Home and Garden Media, New York Times, Art New England, The Boston Globe, Cape Cod Life, Cape Cod Antiques and Art, Fine Life of America, Trenton Times. Collections include: New Jersey State Museum, Scudder,Stevens and Clark, Provincetown Art Ass., Robert Motherwell, Bank of Boston, Polo Inc., Deloitte & Touche, Diane Sawyer and Mike Nichols, Hill Holiday Advertising and Oglethorpe University Museum, Atlanta GA. Major Retrospective May 2006 Cape Cod Museum of Fine Art.

Hambis TSAGGARIS A series of silkscreen prints by Cypriot artist Hambis TSAGGARIS (b. 1947). The works include: Goblins – “Καλικάντζαροι”, 2006; The goblins and the game – “Οι καλικάντζαροι τζιαι το παιξίμιν”, 2005; The goblins are cutting the tree – “Οι καλικάντζαροι κόφκουν το δεντρόν”, 2006; Goblins – “Καλικάντζαροι”, 2006; Goblins – “Καλικάντζαροι”, 2006; Goblins – “Καλικάντζαροι”, 2006. Each print is a unique representation of Cypriot folklore, created using silkscreen techniques.

6491 Picasso: Master of Multiple We are pleased to present our second auction dedicated solely to the editions of Pablo Picasso. Picasso was committed to experimentation and pushing the boundaries of the media with which he worked. This tightly curated auction includes etchings, lithographs, linocuts, and ceramics. Unequivocally an artistic genius, nothing so perfectly expresses his incredible range of talent than presenting a broad range of his editions in one venue.

Picador, 1959 Lot 1 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881-1973) Picador, 1959 linocut in colors on Arches paper a proof, inscribed 'le 7.10.59 à tirer', '1ère épreuve', '5.10.59' in pencil, inkstamped 'Imprimerie Arnéra Archives/Non Signé', verso 20 7/8 x 25 1/4 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Pichet Yan (A.R. 140), 1952 Lot 2 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881–1973) Pichet Yan (A.R. 140), 1952 red earthenware clay turned pitcher, decorations in engobes from the edition of 400; with 'Madoura Plein Feu' and 'Edition Picasso' stamps on the underside Height: 10 1/4 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Portrait de Françoise aux Cheveux Flous, 1947 Lot 13 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881-1973) Portrait de Françoise aux Cheveux Flous, 1947. Aquatint on Arches paper. Signed and inscribed 'épreuve d'artiste' in pencil; one of about 15 artist proofs aside from the edition of 50. 24 1/8 x 17 3/4 inches. This lot is located in Cincinnati.

Jacqueline, en Maja nue, avec la Célestine (from Séries 347), 1968 Lot 14 Hand-signed Pablo Picasso etching, 33/50. Exquisite craft, California collector provenance. Rare opportunity to own a piece of art history valued $15K-$25K.

Homme à la pipe assis, Maja et Célestine (from Série 347), 1968 Lot 15 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881–1973) Homme à la pipe assis, Maja et Célestine (from Série 347), 1968 etching and aquatint on wove paper signed and numbered 32/50 in pencil 2 3/8 x 3 3/8 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Le Cirque (from La Suite Vollard), 1933 Lot 16 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881–1973) Le Cirque (from La Suite Vollard), 1933 etching on laid paper signed in pencil; from the edition of 50 with wide margins 7 7/8 x 11 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Minotaure attaquant une Amazone (from La Suite Vollard), 1933 Lot 17 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881-1973) Minotaure attaquant une Amazone (from La Suite Vollard), 1933 etching on Montval laid paper with Picasso watermark signed in pencil; from the edition of 260 7 5/8 x 10 5/8 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Les Trois Grâces II, 1923 Lot 18 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881–1973) Les Trois Grâces II, 1923 etching on Rives BFK paper signed and numbered 65/100 in pencil 12 7/8 x 7 7/8 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.

Les Saltimbanques (from La Suite des Saltimbanques), 1905 Lot 19 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881–1973) Les Saltimbanques (from La Suite des Saltimbanques), 1905 drypoint on wove paper signed and dated in the plate, from the edition of 250 11 1/4 x 13 inches. This lot is located in Chicago.

Picador, 1959 Lot 1 Pablo Picasso (Spanish, 1881-1973) Picador, 1959 linocut in colors on Arches paper a proof, inscribed 'le 7.10.59 à tirer', '1ère épreuve', '5.10.59' in pencil, inkstamped 'Imprimerie Arnéra Archives/Non Signé', verso 20 7/8 x 25 1/4 inches. This lot is located in Philadelphia.
